title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR DEHYDRATED FRUITS AND VEGETABLES INCLUDING EDIBLE FUNGI (CXC 5-1971)

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR DEHYDRATED FRUITS AND VEGETABLES INCLUDING EDIBLE FUNGI (CXC 5-1971)
sections.summary: Document title only: Code of Hygienic Practice for Dehydrated Fruits and Vegetables including Edible Fungi (CXC 5‑1971).

Scope: applies to artificially dehydrated (including freeze‑dried and some sun‑dried) fruits and vegetables in various forms (sliced, diced, whole, etc.), lists typical covered fruits and vegetables, and excludes tree nuts, high‑moisture dried fruits edible in the dry state, cereal grains, pulses, spices and other dry products.
Defines dehydratation as removal of moisture by artificial means, sometimes combined with sun‑drying.

Raw material requirements covering: (A) environmental sanitation in growing/production areas (sanitary waste disposal, safe irrigation water, regulated pest/disease control); (B) sanitary harvesting and production (equipment/containers not to be hazards, sanitary techniques, segregation/disposal of unfit material, protection from contamination); and (C) transportation (conveyances adequate, cleanable and maintained to avoid contaminating the product).

sections.section: A. Environmental Sanitation in Growing and Food Production Areas
sections.summary: Environmental sanitation requirements: ensure sanitary disposal of human and animal wastes, use irrigation water that does not pose a public health hazard, and apply pest/plant disease control measures only per appropriate official agency recommendations under qualified supervision to avoid toxic residues.

sections.section: B. Sanitary Harvesting and Food Production
sections.summary: Sanitary harvesting and food production requirements: equipment and containers must not be health hazards and reused containers must be cleanable, operations should be carried out in a clean and sanitary manner, unfit materials should be segregated and disposed to prevent contamination, and precautions must be taken to protect raw product from animals, insects, chemicals or microbiological contaminants.

Transportation requirement: conveyances used to move harvested crop or raw product must be adequate for purpose, of material and construction that permit thorough cleaning, and be cleaned and maintained so as not to contaminate the product.

sections.section: SECTION IV - PLANT FACILITIES AND OPERATING REQUIREMENTS
sections.summary: Plant facilities and operating requirements: buildings and layout must permit sanitary operation and protection from contaminants and include sanitary facilities (process separation, potable water per WHO standards, potable ice, separate auxiliary water lines, approved plumbing/waste disposal, adequate lighting/ventilation, toilets and hand‑washing); equipment/utensils must be of suitable materials and sanitary design and drying equipment must not adversely affect product; hygienic operating practices must ensure maintenance, vermin control, exclusion of domestic animals, personnel health controls, proper storage/use of toxic substances, and personnel hygiene; and operating/production requirements cover raw material acceptance, inspection/sorting, washing with potable water, processing/packaging under sanitary conditions, and preservation measures including Codex‑approved chemical treatments provided residue tolerances in commodity standards are not exceeded.

Plant construction and layout details: buildings and surroundings must be free of odours/contaminants, properly sized and maintained to exclude pests and permit cleaning; processes should be separated, potable water and ice provided, auxiliary non‑potable lines isolated, plumbing and waste disposal approved by the official agency, wastes removed to avoid contamination, and adequate lighting, ventilation, toilets and hand‑washing facilities must be provided and kept sanitary.

Specifies that the building and surrounding area for food handling should be located, sized and constructed to avoid objectionable odours, smoke, dust and other contaminants, be large enough to prevent crowding, be in good repair, exclude insects, birds and vermin, permit easy cleaning, and where airborne pollutants are high use equipment to remove pollutants from air blown across or through the product.

Sets sanitary facility and control requirements: separate raw/material storage areas from final preparation and separate edible from inedible areas; provide ample hot and cold potable water meeting WHO 1971 standards and ice from potable water; keep non‑potable lines completely separate and identified; ensure plumbing and waste systems are adequate, watertight and approved; remove solid/semi‑solid wastes continuously and dispose so they cannot contaminate food or harbour vermin; provide adequate lighting and ventilation with protected fixtures over food; provide well‑lit, ventilated, self‑closing toilets that do not open directly into food areas and include hand‑washing facilities and notices; and provide convenient hand‑washing and drying facilities (single‑use towels recommended) kept sanitary.

Requires equipment and utensils in food plants to have suitable materials and sanitary design: food contact surfaces must be appropriate and cleanable, equipment must be designed and installed to prevent hygienic hazards and allow thorough cleaning, utensils for inedible or contaminating materials must be identified and not used for edible products, and drying equipment must be constructed and operated so the drying medium cannot adversely affect the product.

Requires all food contact surfaces to be smooth, free of pits/crevices/loose scale, nontoxic, unaffected by foods, able to withstand repeated cleaning, and non‑absorbent unless a specific acceptable process necessitates use of materials like wood.

sections.section: B. (2) Sanitary design, construction and installation
sections.summary: Requires equipment and utensils to be designed, constructed and installed to prevent hygienic hazards and permit easy, thorough cleaning, with stationary equipment installed to allow cleaning.
States equipment and utensils used for inedible or contaminating materials must be identified and not used for handling edible products.
Requires drying equipment to be constructed and operated so that the drying medium cannot adversely affect the product.

Outlines hygienic operating requirements: keep plant, equipment and premises in good repair and sanitary condition with frequent waste removal and appropriate detergents/disinfectants; implement effective vermin control and exclude domestic animals; ensure personnel with infectious diseases or infected wounds do not work where they could contaminate food; store and use toxic substances (rodenticides, fumigants, insecticides) in locked areas and only under trained supervision; and require high personal cleanliness, appropriate clean clothing and headdress, frequent hand‑washing, prohibition of spitting/eating/tobacco in food areas, treatment and waterproof dressing of minor hand wounds, provision of first‑aid, and use and maintenance of sanitary impermeable gloves except where inappropriate.

sections.section: C. (1) Sanitary maintenance of plant, facilities and premises
sections.summary: Requires the plant building, equipment, utensils and other facilities to be kept in good repair, clean and orderly with frequent waste removal and use of detergents/disinfectants appropriate to purpose and safe for public health.
Requires effective measures to prevent entrance and harbourage of insects, rodents, birds or other vermin on the premises.
States domestic animals (dogs, cats and others) should be excluded from areas where food is processed or stored.

sections.section: 4. (a) All persons working in a food plant should maintain a high degree of personal cleanliness while on  duty.    Clothing  including  suitable  headdress  should  be  appropriate  to  the  duties  being performed and should be kept clean.
sections.summary: Requires food plant workers to maintain high personal cleanliness and clean appropriate clothing/headdress, wash hands as needed, prohibit spitting/eating/tobacco in food areas, take precautions to prevent contamination, appropriately treat and waterproof‑dress minor hand wounds with first‑aid available, and use clean, sanitary impermeable gloves unless incompatible with the work.

sections.section: D. Operating Practices and Production Requirements
sections.summary: Sets operating and production requirements covering raw material acceptance, storage and water use, inspection/sorting, washing/preparation, processing timing to prevent contamination and spoilage, packaging and preservation, and storage/transport: raw materials known to be decomposed/toxic/extraneous not to be accepted and stored to prevent contamination; inspection and washing with potable or suitably treated water; packaging materials stored sanitary and packaging done to preclude contamination; preservation to eliminate insects/mites and prevent deterioration; and finished products stored/transported to avoid contamination, infestation and spoilage.

States raw materials should be rejected if known to contain decomposed, toxic or extraneous substances not removable by normal sorting, stored to protect against contamination and infestation and minimize deterioration, and conveyed with water from a safe or suitably treated source used only with official permission.

Requires raw materials to be inspected, sorted or culled before or within the processing line to remove unfit materials, with such operations carried out in a clean and sanitary manner so only clean, sound materials enter processing.

Requires raw materials to be washed as needed to remove soil or contamination, with wash water not recirculated unless suitably treated and water used for washing, rinsing or conveying final products being of potable quality.

Requires preparatory and packaging operations to be timed to permit expeditious handling of consecutive production units under conditions that prevent contamination, deterioration, spoilage, or development of infectious or toxigenic microorganisms.

Requires packaging materials to be stored clean and sanitary and not to transmit objectionable substances beyond limits acceptable to the official agency, and mandates packaging be done under conditions that preclude introducing contamination into the product.

Requires preservation methods that eliminate insects/mites after processing and protect against contamination, deterioration or public‑health hazards and ensure suitable moisture for storage; permits additional drying and allows treatment with Codex‑approved chemical preservatives provided residues do not exceed commodity tolerances, heat processing, or hermetic sealing to ensure safety under normal non‑refrigerated storage, and mandates storage/transport conditions (time, temperature, humidity, atmosphere) to prevent deterioration and infestation with options for in‑situ or external fumigation and use of cold storage where appropriate.

Recommends each plant designate a single individual (preferably not in production) responsible for cleanliness, maintain a permanent trained sanitation staff skilled in cleaning and equipment disassembly, and identify critical areas/equipment/materials for attention in a permanent sanitation schedule.

Advises that each plant should maintain access to laboratory controls for sanitary quality of processed foods (in addition to official agency controls), with the scope varying by product and management, requires rejecting foods unfit for human consumption, and mandates use of recognized/standard analytical methods so results are interpretable.

Specifies end-product sampling and analysis requirements: products should be, to the extent practicable under good manufacturing practice, free from objectionable matter, must not contain pathogenic microorganisms or microbial-origin toxic substances, and must comply with Codex pesticide residues and food additives requirements referenced in commodity standards.
